nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; turbid inshore reefs among mangroves, fringing-reef drop-offs, and lower estuaries; tolerates or even prefers low salinities. Juveniles tend to settle in seagrass beds. Typically reported from the surface down to roughly 25 m.

diet.notes: Primarily grazes on benthic macroalgae and microalgae. In aquariums, marine algae sheets (nori), algae wafers, spirulina-based frozen foods, and vegetable-based prepared foods are generally recommended. Consistent algal feeding several times per week has been reported to reduce coral-nipping risk in reef tanks.

summary: A large Indo-Pacific rabbitfish with a brown body, scattered orange-gold spots, and a distinctive yellow blotch near the tail. Generally peaceful and a capable algae grazer, but armed with venomous dorsal and anal spines that should be handled with caution.
careNotes:
The Yellow Blotch Rabbitfish (Siganus guttatus), also widely sold as the Goldlined Rabbitfish, Goldlined Spinefoot, or Orange-spotted Spinefoot, is a large reef-associated rabbitfish from the Eastern Indian Ocean and Western Pacific. Adults are commonly reported around 25 cm but have been documented to reach roughly 42 cm TL in the wild, so it is best suited to spacious systems.

Tank size: trade sources differ widely. LiveAquaria advises around 130 US gal (~490 L), Reeflings cites 300 L (clearly low for a fish of this adult size), and ReefApp recommends roughly 1,200 L. Averaging the reasonable values and biasing toward the larger end (per the tank-size cautionary rule), a minimum of about 700 L is used here for a single adult. Aquarists report this species is an active swimmer that benefits from ample open swimming space and a long tank footprint.

Temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22–26°C, FishBase wild data gives 24–28°C, and most hobby sources cluster around 24–26°C; the averaged range used here is 23–27°C, with optimal aquarium temperatures commonly cited around 24–26°C.

Reef safety: generally considered reef-safe when consistently well-fed. Underfed specimens have been known to nip at soft corals and LPS, and macroalgae will be grazed.

Venomous spines: all dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and can deliver a painful sting. Handling should always be done with a net or aquarium tools — never bare hands.

Conspecific aggression: usually peaceful toward unrelated species but tends to be intolerant of other rabbitfish and Siganidae. A single specimen per system is generally advised unless a confirmed mated pair is being maintained in a very large tank.

Brackish tolerance: unlike most marine reef fish, S. guttatus tolerates (and in juveniles, even prefers) low salinities and is often encountered in estuarine and mangrove habitats. Standard reef-aquarium salinity (SG ~1.021–1.025) is still appropriate for captive keeping.

Behavior: schools throughout life in the wild (adult schools commonly around 10–15 individuals); FishBase notes the species is unusually "active at night" for a rabbitfish, suggesting some crepuscular/nocturnal feeding. In captivity it is typically active during the day.

Color change: may adopt a mottled, less vibrant pattern when stressed, sleeping, or frightened — this is normal camouflage and not an indication of illness.

Breeding: wild spawning has been reported around lunar cycles (typically at midnight); reliable captive breeding in home aquariums has not been documented.
breedingNotes: Wild spawning has been reported around lunar cycles, typically at midnight, with the species spawning in groups. Larvae are planktonic. Reliable captive breeding in home aquariums has not been documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page resolved from the foxface-rabbitfish collection URL. Confirmed adult size ~10 in, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 130 gal minimum, moderate care, peaceful (except with other rabbitfish), reef-safe with caution, herbivorous diet, venomous dorsal spines.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (max 42 cm TL, common 25 cm TL), taxonomy (Siganidae, Acanthuriformes), temperature 24–28°C, depth 0–25 m, native range, trophic level 2.7, schooling behavior (adult schools of ~10–15), and venomous spines. Temperature spread vs LiveAquaria (22–26°C) averaged to 23–27°C.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed max size 42 cm, peaceful except toward conspecifics, hardy/easy care, herbivorous diet, venomous spines, and the species' color-change-when-frightened behavior. Recommended minimum tank ~1,200 L — much larger than LiveAquaria's 130 gal, included in the tank-size averaging that landed on 700 L.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Provided 35 cm max length, 24–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, moderate-to-easy care, semi-aggressive toward conspecifics, herbivorous diet, and wild schooling behavior. Their 300 L minimum-tank figure was disregarded as too low for a 35–42 cm fish.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Confirmed family Siganidae, 76–80°F, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.023–1.026, very peaceful except with other rabbitfish, reef-safe if well-fed, venomous dorsal spines. Tank-size and lifespan not provided.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild spawning has been reported around lunar cycles, typically at midnight, with the species spawning in groups. Larvae are planktonic. Reliable captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.
